title: Was It Worth It? The True Cost of Trump’s Iran War | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Max Boot argues the 2026 Iran War, ended by an MOU, cost roughly $132B to U.S. consumers and taxpayers while failing to achieve regime change or disarmament. Iran emerged with its regime, arsenal, and 11 tons of enriched uranium intact, plus a proven ability to choke the Strait of Hormuz and a $300B reconstruction fund.

insights.claim: Moody's Analytics estimates the 2026 Iran War cost U.S. consumers and taxpayers about $132 billion so far, with the largest share coming through the price at the pump.
insights.speaker: Max Boot
insights.so_what: Quantifies the consumer/inflation hit from the conflict; pump prices are the main transmission channel to watch.
insights.quote: Moody’s Analytics estimates that the war has cost U.S. consumers and taxpayers about $132 billion so far and counting.
insights.locator: Then there is the economic cost.

insights.claim: U.S. gasoline prices peaked at an average of $4.56 a gallon during the Iran War before dropping once a deal appeared likely, while fertilizer prices climbed about 47 percent, indirectly raising food costs.
insights.speaker: Max Boot
insights.so_what: Fertilizer's 47% spike links the conflict to a food-inflation channel beyond energy; watch agricultural input costs.
insights.quote: Gasoline prices peaked at an average of $4.56 a gallon before dropping once it appeared that the two sides might come to an agreement. Fertilizer prices also climbed about 47 percent during the war
insights.locator: Then there is the economic cost.

insights.claim: As a result of the war, the World Bank downgraded its global growth forecast for the year to 2.5 percent, the lowest level since the Covid-19 pandemic.
insights.speaker: Max Boot
insights.so_what: A war-driven global growth cut to 2.5% reframes the macro backdrop for risk assets and cyclicals.
insights.quote: the World Bank downgraded its global forecast for economic growth this year to 2.5 percent—the lowest level since the Covid-19 pandemic.
insights.locator: Then there is the economic cost.

themes.theme: Strait of Hormuz as economic weapon

insights.claim: Iran demonstrated it could close the Strait of Hormuz to all but a trickle of traffic using drones, missiles and mines, and because the Strait normally carries 20 percent of the world's oil, the closure plunged the world into an energy crisis.
insights.speaker: Max Boot
insights.so_what: A proven low-cost Hormuz closure capability is a recurring oil tail risk; price persistent geopolitical premium into energy.
insights.quote: using drones, missiles and mines, it was able to close the Strait of Hormuz to all but a trickle of the usual maritime traffic. Because the Strait normally carries 20 percent of the world’s oil, its closure plunged the world into an energy crisis

insights.claim: Under the MOU Iran pledged free access through the Strait of Hormuz for only sixty days, after which it is expected to charge 'user fees' amounting to tolls in coordination with Oman, violating the principle of freedom of navigation.
insights.speaker: Max Boot
insights.so_what: The 60-day window is a hard catalyst date for renewed shipping-cost and oil risk; monitor for toll imposition.
insights.quote: Iran only pledged to allow free access through the critical waterway for sixty days. Tehran has given every indication that it will then try to charge, in coordination with Oman, “user fees” that amount to tolls by another name.
insights.locator: The generous terms of the MOU

insights.claim: The U.S. fired more than one thousand Tomahawk cruise missiles and over 1,500 air-defense missiles in the Iran War, expending more than half of its prewar Patriot missile inventory.
insights.speaker: Max Boot
insights.so_what: Massive munitions drawdown is a multi-year tailwind for defense primes supplying missiles and interceptors.
insights.quote: The U.S. fired more than one thousand Tomahawk cruise missiles and more than 1,500 air-defense missiles. The expended munitions include more than half of the prewar inventory of Patriot missiles
insights.locator: It will be even harder and costlier

insights.claim: Replacing depleted U.S. missile stockpiles could take up to six years, leaving the United States less prepared for a major conflict against China or Russia and short of Patriots needed by Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Max Boot
insights.so_what: A six-year restocking gap weakens U.S. deterrence and Ukraine air defense, raising secondary conflict and defense-spend risk.
insights.quote: Replacing those stockpiles could take up to six years, the Wall Street Journal reports, and in the meantime the United States will be less prepared for a major conflict against China or Russia.
insights.locator: It will be even harder and costlier

insights.claim: Despite bombing setbacks, Iran emerged from the war with most of its drones and missiles intact and still retains an estimated eleven tons of enriched uranium along with bomb-making know-how.
insights.speaker: Max Boot
insights.so_what: Retained enrichment stock and arsenal mean proliferation risk and renewed-conflict odds remain elevated post-deal.
insights.quote: the regime still retains an estimated eleven tons of enriched uranium along with bomb-making know-how and capacity.
insights.locator: Other states in the region also suffered

insights.claim: The author contends the Iran War delivered little tangible benefit and may embolden Iran, which can use unfrozen assets and a $300 billion reconstruction fund to rebuild its military and increase support for proxies like Hezbollah.
insights.speaker: Max Boot
insights.so_what: A cash-rearmed Iran sustains regional risk; treat the deal as a pause, not resolution, for Middle East exposure.
insights.quote: Iran can now use the funds it receives from the lifting of sanctions and unfreezing of assets to rebuild not only its economy but also its military capacity—and to increase support for proxy groups like Hezbollah.
insights.locator: In sum, the Iran war cost a lot
